title: IICRC S500:2025 Overview — Key Changes from the 2021 Edition & What They Mean for Australian Restorers
description: A comprehensive overview of the IICRC S500:2025 Standard for Professional Water Damage Restoration — what changed from 2021, why the changes matter, and how Australian restoration technicians should update their practice. Referenced by RestoreAssist throughout.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## IICRC S500: The Foundational Standard

The IICRC S500 Standard for Professional Water Damage Restoration is the primary technical reference for the water damage restoration industry globally, including Australia. It defines categories and classes of water damage, methodologies for assessment, drying, and documentation, and the professional responsibilities of the restorer.

The 2025 edition supersedes the 2021 edition. Understanding what changed — and why — is important for practitioners who may still be referencing older training or documentation templates.

## Key Changes in S500:2025

### Revised Category Definitions

The 2025 edition clarified the Category 2 definition to address the increasingly common scenario of water damage from appliance discharges. Category 2 now explicitly includes water that has contacted biofilm-colonised surfaces (such as the interior of washing machine drums or dishwasher sumps) even when the original water source was clean. This has practical implications for scope writing on appliance-related losses.

### Updated Drying Goal Language

S500:2025 §11.4 introduced more prescriptive language around drying goal documentation. The restorer is now explicitly required to establish the drying goal in writing at the commencement of the job, document the basis for the dry standard selected, and confirm achievement on two consecutive monitoring visits. This formalises what was previously common practice but not explicitly required.

### Equipment Performance Criteria

Appendix B in the 2025 edition was significantly revised to account for LGR dehumidifiers as the baseline unit (replacing conventional refrigerant). The revised tables give more accurate capacity recommendations for mid-to-late drying phase conditions when GPP values are lower.

### Psychrometric Documentation Requirements

The 2025 edition expanded the documentation requirements to include psychrometric readings (temperature, relative humidity, and GPP or specific humidity) alongside material moisture content readings. Previous editions referenced psychrometrics as best practice; S500:2025 makes them a required component of daily monitoring records.

### Expanded Class 4 Guidance

Class 4 materials received expanded treatment in the 2025 edition, with specific guidance on desiccant dehumidification requirements, extended drying timelines, and the criteria for distinguishing Class 4 from Class 2 with dense material involvement. This addresses a common grey area in previous editions.

### Updated Antimicrobial Treatment Language

Section 7.6 was revised to align with current EPA guidance on antimicrobial product use. The 2025 edition emphasises that antimicrobial treatments do not substitute for physical removal of contaminated materials and that product claims must be supported by EPA-registered label instructions.

## What This Means for Australian Practitioners

Australian insurers and courts will increasingly expect restoration reports to reference S500:2025 rather than the 2021 edition. Documentation templates, scope libraries, and training materials should be reviewed against the updated standard.

The most immediately practical changes for Australian restorers are the enhanced drying goal documentation requirements and the revised equipment capacity tables. Both affect day-to-day documentation and should be reflected in standard operating procedures.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. The 2025 edition of IICRC S500 is now the current standard, and today I want to give you a clear picture of what changed from 2021 and what you need to update in your practice.

First: why does this matter? Australian insurers and courts reference the current edition of S500. If your documentation templates and scope practices are still based on the 2021 edition, you may be citing superseded guidance. Let's fix that.

Let me run through the key changes.

The Category 2 definition was revised. The 2025 edition now explicitly includes water that has contacted biofilm-colonised surfaces — think the inside of a washing machine drum or dishwasher sump — even when the original water was clean. If you have an appliance-related loss and the water has been sitting in that machine, it may qualify as Category 2 under the 2025 edition even though the supply line water was clean. This directly affects scope — particularly antimicrobial treatment decisions.

Drying goal documentation. Section 11.4 is now prescriptive. You must document the drying goal in writing at the start of the job, state the dry standard you're working toward and why, and confirm achievement on two consecutive monitoring visits. This was always best practice, but the 2025 edition makes it a requirement. If your standard operating procedure doesn't already include this, add it now.

Equipment capacity tables. Appendix B was significantly revised to use LGR dehumidifiers as the baseline — not conventional refrigerant. The updated tables give more accurate recommendations for mid-to-late drying conditions. If you're still calculating quantities from the old tables, you're working from outdated data.

Psychrometric readings. Temperature, relative humidity, and GPP are now required components of daily monitoring records — not just best practice. Your daily logs need to include all three, not just material moisture content.

Class 4 materials got expanded guidance. The 2025 edition clarifies the criteria for distinguishing Class 4 from Class 2 with dense material involvement, and gives specific guidance on desiccant requirements and extended drying timelines.

In RestoreAssist, all scope templates, drying goal validation, and equipment calculations reference S500:2025. So if you're working through the platform, you're already aligned with the current standard.

Head to restoreassist.com.au to learn more.
